title: Odyssey, Book I: The Counsel of the Gods
description: The opening of the Odyssey: the gods debate Odysseus' fate, Athena descends to Ithaca.
introduction: Where the Iliad begins with wrath, the Odyssey begins with wandering. Homer's second epic follows Odysseus — polytropos, the man of many turns — on his decade-long journey home from Troy. Book I opens on Olympus, where Zeus delivers one of the most important theological statements in Greek literature: humans bring suffering upon themselves beyond what fate decrees. Athena then descends to Ithaca to set the homecoming in motion.

translation.original_text: Ἄνδρα μοι ἔννεπε, Μοῦσα, πολύτροπον, ὃς μάλα πολλὰ πλάγχθη, ἐπεὶ Τροίης ἱερὸν πτολίεθρον ἔπερσε· πολλῶν δ᾿ ἀνθρώπων ἴδεν ἄστεα καὶ νόον ἔγνω, πολλὰ δ᾿ ὅ γ᾿ ἐν πόντῳ πάθεν ἄλγεα ὃν κατὰ θυμόν, ἀρνύμενος ἥν τε ψυχὴν καὶ νόστον ἑταίρων.
translation.text: Tell me, Muse, of the man of many turns, who wandered far and wide after he sacked the sacred city of Troy. Many were the peoples whose cities he saw and whose minds he learned, and many the sorrows he suffered at sea in his heart, struggling for his own life and the homecoming of his companions.

translation.original_text: ὢ πόποι, οἷον δή νυ θεοὺς βροτοὶ αἰτιόωνται. ἐξ ἡμέων γάρ φασι κάκ᾿ ἔμμεναι· οἱ δὲ καὶ αὐτοὶ σφῇσιν ἀτασθαλίῃσιν ὑπὲρ μόρον ἄλγε᾿ ἔχουσιν.
translation.text: How mortals blame the gods! They say that evils come from us, but they themselves by their own recklessness bring suffering beyond what is fated.
